title: Optional rolling-window savings suffix for the statusline
author: andrebrait
body:
## Motivation

The statusline suffix (`⛏ 346.0k`) written by `caveman-stats.js` is a lifetime total — `aggregateHistory(historyPath, null)`. Once the number gets big it barely moves, so as a statusline signal it goes stale: you can't see what caveman saved you *today*.

The stats engine already has everything needed for a windowed number: `/caveman-stats --since 24h` works, and `aggregateHistory(historyPath, sinceMs)` takes the window directly.

## Proposal

An opt-in env knob, e.g. `CAVEMAN_STATUSLINE_SINCE=24h`: when set, `caveman-stats.js` writes the suffix from `aggregateHistory(historyPath, sinceMs)` instead of the lifetime aggregate (perhaps rendered as `⛏ 12.4k/24h`). Default behavior unchanged.

## Context

I have a rolling variant working locally — an external sampler over the per-session snapshots in `.caveman-history.jsonl` that renders a delta over a configurable window next to the `[CAVEMAN]` badge — and day-to-day it's a much more interesting number than the lifetime total. Doing it natively via the existing `--since` path would be cleaner than my out-of-tree version. I don't know whether you want this in the plugin, but if so I'm happy to open a PR.

title: Pre-tool-call narration lines emitted in English during Chinese sessions, despite full-mode's "no tool-call narration" rule
author: serenNan
body:
## Environment
- Claude Code CLI, plugin install (`claude plugin marketplace add JuliusBrussee/caveman` + `claude plugin install caveman@caveman`, scope: user)
- caveman: main @ 0d95a81
- caveman level: `full`
- Session language: Chinese throughout (all user prompts Chinese)

## Issue
With caveman `full` active in an all-Chinese session, the final substantive answer to the user is correctly written in Chinese (per the Rules-section promise "Preserve user's dominant language … Compress the style, not the language"). But the short one-line status statement emitted immediately before each tool call (the harness convention of stating one sentence before invoking a tool) comes out in English, or English-verb + Chinese-noun mixed — even though it's the same turn, same model, same session.

Observed in a real (unrelated) project session:
- Before a Bash call: `Verify. Check EDI code carrier分支.`
- Before another Bash call: `Read send dialog + G1/G2/G4 guard 看是否真共用同一发送机制。`
- The turn's actual answer bullet, fully Chinese: `不是"完全没变化只是PDF不一样"。发送机制共用一套，但有实质分支。`

Note: caveman `full` mode's own Intensity table explicitly says **"No tool-call narration"** — so arguably these lines shouldn't be emitted at all, in any language. Their presence suggests the narration-suppression rule isn't reliably honored, and when narration does slip through, it isn't covered by the language-preservation rule either (that rule's wording targets "reply", and this status line may not register as "the reply" to the model).

## Hypothesis
The pre-tool-call narration is a harness-level convention independent of SKILL.md, so SKILL.md's language-preservation rule doesn't obviously apply to it. When caveman `full` compresses it into terse fragments, the compression pattern likely borrows sentence structure from SKILL.md's own English-only examples (e.g. "Bug in auth middleware. Fix:") — same general mechanism as #679 and #665 (English-only in-context example text biasing output under compression pressure), but a different specific surface (tool narration, not the reply body or the Auto-Clarity block).

## Suggested fix
Either:
1. Extend the language-preservation rule's scope to explicitly cover any auxiliary/status text the model emits, not just the final reply, or
2. Reinforce "No tool-call narration" so this text is suppressed entirely at `full`/`ultra`, sidestepping the language question.

Related: #679 (Auto-Clarity English block leak), #665 (named-language examples risk drift under compaction) — same class of bug, different surface.

title: bug: shell install command fails on `npm` >=12
author: madkarmaa
body:
**What happen**

Ran the [shell install command](https://github.com/JuliusBrussee/caveman#install) and it failed:

```log
npm error code EALLOWGIT
npm error Fetching packages of type "git" have been disabled
npm error Refusing to fetch "github:JuliusBrussee/caveman"
npm error A complete log of this run can be found in: /home/user/.npm/_logs/2026-07-11T14_01_09_335Z-debug-0.log
```

**Reason**

```bash
> npm -v
12.0.0
```

> - _allow-git and allow-remote now default to "none"; set them to "all" (or "root") to install git or user-supplied tarball-URL dependencies._

**[Source](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v12/using-npm/changelog#%EF%B8%8F-breaking-changes)**

**Fix**

macOS · Linux · WSL · Git Bash
```bash
export NPM_CONFIG_ALLOW_GIT=all
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JuliusBrussee/caveman/main/install.sh | bash
unset NPM_CONFIG_ALLOW_GIT
```

Windows · PowerShell 5.1+
```pwsh
$env:NPM_CONFIG_ALLOW_GIT = "all"
irm https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JuliusBrussee/caveman/main/install.ps1 | iex
Remove-Item Env:NPM_CONFIG_ALLOW_GIT
```

**Platform**
- [ ] Claude Code
- [x] Codex
- [x] Other: global (`~/.agents`)

**Version / install method**
Latest / [shell install command](https://github.com/JuliusBrussee/caveman#install)

title: caveman-compress: missing encoding="utf-8" on file I/O corrupts non-ASCII files on Windows (data loss, no crash)
author: Jisung-Jeong
body:
## Summary

On Windows, `skills/caveman-compress/scripts/compress.py` (and `validate.py`, `detect.py`) call `Path.read_text()` / `Path.write_text()` without an explicit `encoding` argument. Python falls back to the OS locale's preferred encoding — on a Korean-locale Windows machine that's `cp949`, not UTF-8.

This silently corrupts any UTF-8 source file containing non-ASCII characters (Korean text, em dashes, arrows, etc.), and in the worst case **truncates the original file to 0 bytes with no usable backup**.

## Root cause

- `filepath.read_text(errors="ignore")` (compress.py:249) decodes UTF-8 bytes as cp949 → mojibake, with `errors="ignore"` silently dropping bytes it can't decode (lossy, not reversible).
- That already-corrupted text is what gets sent to the LLM for compression and what gets written to the backup file.
- `filepath.write_text(compressed)` (compress.py:313) encodes the LLM's compressed output back with the locale codec. `Path.write_text` opens the file in `'w'` mode, which **truncates immediately on open**. If the compressed text contains a character not representable in the locale codec (e.g. an em dash `—`, common in the LLM's own output style), `str.encode()` raises `UnicodeEncodeError` mid-write — after truncation, before any bytes are flushed. Net effect: the original file is left at **0 bytes**, the exception bubbles up to `cli.py`'s generic `except Exception` handler, and the user sees a generic `❌ Error: 'cp949' codec can't encode character ...` with no indication the source file was just destroyed.
- `validate.py`'s `read_file()` has the same missing-encoding bug when comparing original vs. compressed.

## Repro

On Windows with a non-English system locale (e.g. Korean, `cp949`):

```powershell
py -m scripts "path\to\some_korean_language.md"
```

Any `.md` file containing Korean text (or other non-cp1252/cp949-safe punctuation) is at risk. In our case this corrupted a project `CLAUDE.md` (truncated to 0 bytes) and a global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (fully mojibake'd, including its on-disk backup, since the backup is written from the already-mis-decoded text) with no clean way to recover — we only recovered because the original text happened to still be visible earlier in the conversation transcript.

## Fix

Explicitly pass `encoding="utf-8"` on every `read_text` / `write_text` call in `compress.py`, `validate.py`, and `detect.py`. This is a one-line change per call site and fully resolves the corruption — verified locally by patching all call sites and re-running the compression successfully on the previously-affected files.

Call sites needing the fix (paths relative to `skills/caveman-compress/scripts/`):
- `compress.py`: `filepath.read_text(...)`, `backup_path.write_text(...)`, `backup_path.read_text(...)`, `filepath.write_text(...)` (×3 call sites total for write_text)
- `validate.py`: `read_file()`
- `detect.py`: the extensionless-file content sniff

## Suggested additional hardening

Independent of the encoding fix, `compress.py`'s backup flow means the *original* file (`filepath`) can be truncated by `Path.write_text()`'s truncate-on-open behavior even when the subsequent encode fails. Consider writing to a temp file in the same directory and renaming (atomic replace) instead of writing directly to `filepath`, so a mid-write encode failure can never leave the user's source file empty.

## Environment

- OS: Windows 11
- Locale: Korean (`cp949`)
- Plugin version: 0.1.0 (per `package.json`)

title: SKILL.md: English-centric drop rules degrade Korean at full/ultra — particle drops flip subject/object; in-context wenyan examples leak hanja into Korean output
author: temeraire97
body:
## Environment
- Claude Code CLI 2.1.205 (`claude -p` single-shot and `--continue` multi-turn chains), macOS (Darwin 24.6.0)
- caveman: main @ 0d95a81
- Models: claude-fable-5, claude-opus-4-8
- Arms per the evals honesty rule (skill vs terse control): skill = `"Answer concisely.\n\n"` + SKILL.md + level line; control = `"Answer concisely."` only

## Issue
Two Rules-section lines interact badly in Korean sessions.

Line 21:

> Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler … Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for").

Line 23:

> Preserve user's dominant language. … Compress the style, not the language.

**Defect 1 — particle drops flip subject/object.** Line 21's drop list is English morphology. Korean has no articles; the nearest small grammatical morphemes are case particles (조사: 이/가/을/를/은/는/에/에서/…), which — unlike articles — carry subject/object case relations. When "drop articles" generalizes onto Korean and "short synonyms" pushes toward a transitive-verb stand-in, compression deletes exactly the morpheme that disambiguates who does what to whom. That is a comprehension break, i.e. a violation of line 23's own promise.

**Defect 2 — hanja leaks into Korean output.** The six wenyan example outputs (lines 47–56) sit in context every session. Under `ultra`'s compression pressure the model occasionally swaps a Korean word for a classical character to shorten (있음 → 有). Korean users experience this as random hanja appearing mid-sentence in otherwise normal Korean answers.

## Reproduction

**Defect 1 (`ultra` + skill).** Prompt: `React 컴포넌트가 계속 리렌더링되는 이유가 뭐야?` — item 1 of the answer, verbatim:

> 1. **부모 리렌더** — 부모 그리면 자식도 그림. `React.memo`로 차단.

`그리다` ("draw", chosen as compressed stand-in for "render") is transitive. With the subject particle dropped, `부모 그리면` reads more naturally as "if (you) draw the parent" than "if the parent renders". The terse control compressed the same fact unambiguously via noun-compound form: `부모 리렌더 → 자식 전부 리렌더`. Skill-induced, not inherent to concise Korean.

**Defect 2 — three evidence tiers.**

1. **Field-condition repro.** Four 12-turn Korean-only conversations (no prompt ever mentions anything Chinese/classical; a per-turn caveman reminder was prefixed to each prompt, mimicking the UserPromptSubmit hook). 48 assistant turns → 1 hanja occurrence (claude-fable-5, turn 6): a cache-invalidation comparison table cell compressing 있음 to a single classical character:

   > `| CDC 이벤트 | 중상 (지연 有) | 높음 | …`

   ≈2% per turn — matches the intermittent, random pattern reported from real usage.
2. **Amplified mechanism repro.** Twenty 2-turn sessions (claude-opus-4-8): turn 1 elicits a wenyan-register answer into history, turn 2 asks a plain Korean question. **16/20** turn-2 outputs contained classical characters — 15 stuck in full wenyan register (a one-off wenyan-style request persists as if the level had been switched), 1 mixed Korean+hanja. With an explicit "이제 평소처럼 한국어로" cue: 0/1. The model readily borrows in-context classical material for Korean answers under compression pressure.
3. **Single-turn negative control.** 29 single-shot skill-arm outputs (lite/full/ultra, both models): 0 hanja. The trigger needs accumulated context — consistent with tier 1's low per-turn rate.

## Not reproduced / honest scope
- `lite`: clean across all runs — its own row scopes the failure away ("Keep articles + full sentences").
- Particle damage is not universal: object-particle drops on intransitive verbs or telegraphic noun+verb pairs read as standard Korean note register, unambiguous. The break needs the transitive-verb + dropped-case-particle interaction.

## Hypothesis
Line 21 is written in English morphology; applied to particle-marked (agglutinative) languages it maps onto case particles that carry grammatical relations articles don't. The wenyan examples supply in-context classical-character material that `ultra`'s "one word when one word enough" pressure occasionally borrows for Korean output; once any classical register enters conversation history, the effect compounds (16/20).

## Suggested fix (PR to follow, ≤10 added lines, pure addition to `skills/caveman/SKILL.md`)
1. Rules section: scope "drop articles" to article languages — particles/case-markers are grammar, keep them; compress politeness/hedging/filler instead. Principle stated generally (no language-name list, per #665's concern), one Korean Not/Yes pair.
2. After the wenyan examples: classical characters belong to wenyan modes only; never swap a word to a classical character to shorten; a one-off classical-style request covers that answer only.

## Relationship to open work
- #658 — complementary: that PR keeps the response language; this fixes compression quality *within* the language. SKILL.md-only, no hook surface.
- #667 — no conflict: line 23 untouched, additions only.
- #665 — the fix deliberately avoids adding language-name tokens to always-in-context text.
- #536 — wenyan untouched, only scoped.

Raw transcripts (67 outputs across all conditions) retained; happy to attach.

title: Auto-Clarity English example block leaks verbatim into non-English sessions
author: temeraire97
body:
## Environment
- Claude Code CLI 2.1.205 (`claude -p`, single-shot), macOS (Darwin 24.6.0)
- caveman: main @ 0d95a81
- Models: claude-opus-4-8, claude-fable-5
- Arms per the evals honesty rule: skill arm = `"Answer concisely.\n\n"` + `skills/caveman/SKILL.md` (+ level line); control = `"Answer concisely."` only

## Issue
The Auto-Clarity section's only concrete output template (SKILL.md lines 69–74) is an English warning block. In a Korean session that triggers Auto-Clarity, the model can reproduce that English template near-verbatim inside an otherwise all-Korean response — violating the Rules-section promise "Preserve user's dominant language. … Compress the style, not the language" (line 23).

## Reproduction (`full` level, Korean)
Prompt: `users 테이블에서 컬럼 드랍하는 마이그레이션인데 지금 실행해도 안전할까?`

The response was Korean throughout (checklist, follow-up questions, particles intact) — except the Auto-Clarity warning, emitted in English, structurally matching the SKILL.md line 70 example:

> **Warning:** Dropping a column permanently deletes that column's data and cannot be undone by re-running the migration. Verify a backup or snapshot exists before running it against any shared or production database.

Intermittent: the same prompt at `ultra` localized correctly — a full-sentence Korean warning ending `Caveman 재개.` (the Korean analog of the example's `Caveman resume.`). Auto-Clarity detection itself fired correctly in both runs; only the output language flips.

## Hypothesis
The English block is the section's only concrete exemplar. Under the Auto-Clarity trigger the model reaches for the nearest template and reproduces it literally, language included.

## Suggested fix
One added line in the Auto-Clarity section marking the example as format-only — the warning must be written in the session language. PR to follow.

Related: #658 keeps the response language generally (complementary — no shared surface; this issue is about the hardcoded example template specifically). Raw transcripts retained, happy to attach.

title: Caveman increases costs rather than saving
author: geofffranks
body:
**What happen**
I ran a test between off, on (across multiple caveman modes), and in each, it seemed that although caveman was saving on output tokens, it was still ultimately costing more.

```
full vs normal costs:
- full: $0.6255 vs normal: $0.5083 → caveman full is 23% MORE EXPENSIVE
- Output tokens only -8.2% but hook overhead is 4.5% of cost

ultra/wenyan-ultra vs normal costs:
- wenyan-ultra: $0.4668 vs normal: $0.4628 → effectively identical
- ultra: $0.5288 vs normal: $0.4628 → ultra is 14% more expensive

Per-prompt pattern:
- BOSH debug: full saves -33.6% output tokens — caveman genuinely compresses troubleshooting prose
- k8s scheduling, Go explain: full generates MORE tokens (+7.5%, +29.5%) — explanatory content gets longer, not shorter
- Short prompts (monorepo): high variance makes results unreliable

Bottom line: caveman reduces tokens on diagnostic/factual responses but increases them on explanatory content. Hook overhead (~500 tokens/turn × cache_creation pricing) is non-trivial enough that the net cost is worse for full and ultra modes, not better.
```

**Expected**

This should save money not spend it? Though I'm not sure if this is a fundamental result of how LLM's work under-the-hood because you're having to spend thinking on altering already-thought text into new text. 


**Platform**
- [x] Claude Code
- [ ] Codex
- [ ] Other: ___

**Version / install method**

title: TrustScope: suggested trust & security improvements for caveman
author: GmanFooFoo
body:
Hi! This is a friendly, optional set of suggestions for **JuliusBrussee/caveman**, generated by [TrustScope](https://trustscope.neckarshore.ai) from the [OpenSSF Scorecard](https://securityscorecards.dev) (v5.5.0).

None of these are required — they are common hardening steps that tend to raise a project's supply-chain and governance signals. Take whatever is useful and ignore the rest.

### Security & Supply Chain
- **Branch Protection** — Add a branch-protection rule: require a pull request, a required status check, and dismiss-stale reviews. (Private repositories need a paid GitHub plan for branch protection.)
- **Code Review** — Require review before merge. Solo projects can satisfy this with an automated reviewer (e.g. CodeRabbit) instead of blocking human approval.
- **Dependency Update Tool** — Add `.github/dependabot.yml` — the `github-actions` ecosystem always, plus `npm`/etc. wherever a manifest exists.
- **Pinned Dependencies** — SHA-pin every `uses:` Action to a full commit SHA (with a version comment), then let Dependabot keep the pins current.
- **SAST** — Add a CodeQL workflow for the repository's real languages (build-mode `none`). Use `languages:` (plural) on `codeql-action/init` — `language:` is silently ignored — and job-scope `security-events: write`. Private repositories need GitHub Advanced Security.

---
_Assessed via TrustScope (https://trustscope.neckarshore.ai) — an open-source trust report by Neckarshore AI. These are suggestions, not demands._

title: caveman-review: AUT shows format lift but 0pp analytical lift
author: ilyazub
body:
## Finding

Applied [Agent Usability Testing (AUT)](https://github.com/serpapi/skills/blob/master/skills/agent-usability-test/SKILL.md) to caveman-review (N=1 pilot, Sonnet 4.6).

## Results

| Criterion | WITH skill | WITHOUT skill |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| Bugs detected (3 planted) | 3/3 | 3/3 |
| Correct line numbers | 3/3 | 0/3 |
| Format: `L<n>: emoji type:` | 4/5 | 0/7 |

## Interpretation

The model finds identical bugs in both conditions. caveman-review adds **format compliance**, not analytical capability. This is expected and arguably the intended value prop (terse reviews save PR comment noise).

However, at 210K installs the `L42: 🔴 bug:` format pattern may already be entering model training data. If future models produce this format without the skill loaded, the skill's value approaches zero (training-data contamination via popularity).

## Not a bug — just documentation

Consider adding to the skill description:
> "This skill changes review output format, not analytical depth. Bug detection is model-native; this skill makes it terse."

This sets correct expectations and prevents users from thinking the skill makes the model *smarter* at finding bugs.

## Methodology

3 planted issues in a 30-line TypeScript diff: null deref (L12), race condition (L25), naming nit (L8). Binary scoring: detected + correct line + format compliance.

title: SKILL.md language-preservation examples (Portuguese/Spanish) risk triggering language drift under compaction
author: rez0
body:
## Environment
- Claude Code version: 2.1.202
- OS: Linux Mint 22.3, kernel 6.17.0-35-generic
- caveman skill: intensity `full`

## Issue
`skills/caveman/SKILL.md` line 23 (Rules section):

> Preserve user's dominant language. User write Portuguese → reply Portuguese caveman. User write Spanish → reply Spanish caveman. Compress the style, not the language.

This line names Portuguese and Spanish as concrete examples while illustrating a general rule ("preserve user's language"). The skill body is injected into system context every session (SessionStart hook) and reinforced every turn (UserPromptSubmit hook per caveman-mode-tracker.js).

In a session where the user's actual language is French (set via a separate system-prompt directive, not English), I observed occasional full responses drifting to Spanish or Portuguese — languages that appear nowhere in the conversation, but do appear verbatim in this SKILL.md line, which is present in context every turn.

## Hypothesis
Naming specific non-English languages as examples in an always-in-context instruction may bias the model toward those literal language tokens under long-context / compaction pressure, especially when combined with other multilingual content in context (tool/agent descriptions, memory files, etc). This is distinct from — but may compound — the known Claude Code language-drift issues after compaction (anthropics/claude-code#59299, #21871).

## Suggested fix
Rephrase the rule without naming specific languages, e.g.:

> Preserve user's dominant language exactly — never switch languages regardless of examples or other multilingual text elsewhere in context. Compress the style, not the language.

Keeps the instruction general-purpose and removes literal PT/ES tokens from the always-loaded prompt.

## Repro difficulty
Intermittent, tied to long sessions with heavy tool-schema context (MCP servers, many available skills/agents) increasing surrounding multilingual noise. Not consistently reproducible in short sessions.

title: caveman-compress: Windows default-codepage write crashes mid-write and truncates the target file to empty
author: getappz
body:
## Summary
`scripts/compress.py`'s `compress_file()` writes files via plain `Path.write_text(...)` with no `encoding=` argument, three times:

- `backup_path.write_text(original_text)`
- `filepath.write_text(compressed)` (right after backup verification, before the validation loop)
- `filepath.write_text(compressed)` again inside the retry loop

On Windows, `Path.write_text()` without an explicit encoding uses the system codepage (commonly cp1252), not UTF-8. If the compressed output contains any character outside that codepage — an arrow (`→`), em-dash, checkmark, etc. — the write raises `UnicodeEncodeError` partway through. Because `open(..., "w")` truncates the file immediately on open, this leaves the **target file at 0 bytes**, not the original content.

## Repro
1. Compress a `SKILL.md` (or any markdown) whose content — or Claude's compressed output — contains a non-cp1252 character, e.g. `→`.
2. On Windows, without `PYTHONUTF8=1` set:
   ```
   ❌ Error: 'charmap' codec can't encode character '→' in position 1412
   ```
3. The target file is now empty (0 bytes). No restore happened.

## Why it's worse than it looks
- The *first* `filepath.write_text(compressed)` call (right after backup verification, before entering the `for attempt in range(MAX_RETRIES)` validation loop) has **no try/except and no restore-on-failure**. The retry loop's own write *is* guarded (`if attempt == MAX_RETRIES - 1: filepath.write_text(original_text); ...`), but that safety net doesn't cover this earlier call site.
- The backup at `backup_dir_for(filepath)` is written successfully before this (it's a separate write of `original_text`, which apparently round-trips fine even containing the same `→` in my repro — so backups are safe), so no permanent data loss *if* the user knows to look for `%LOCALAPPDATA%\caveman-compress\backups\<dir>\<stem>.original.md` and restore manually. But the CLI's own error message gives no indication the file was truncated or where the backup lives (it does print the backup path — but only on a *successful* run's completion message, not on this crash path).
- `cli.py` already reconfigures `sys.stdout`/`sys.stderr` to UTF-8 for print statements (per its own comment referencing issue #152), but that doesn't touch the plain `write_text()`/`read_text()` calls in `compress.py`, which is the actual crash site here.

## Suggested fix
1. Pass `encoding="utf-8"` explicitly on every `read_text()`/`write_text()` call in `compress.py` (backup write, both primary writes, and the original read). This is the root fix — don't rely on the caller having `PYTHONUTF8=1` set.
2. Wrap the pre-validation-loop `filepath.write_text(compressed)` in the same try/except + restore-from-backup pattern the retry loop already has, so a crash there can't leave the target file truncated.
3. On any write failure, print the backup path so the user isn't left guessing where their content went.

Confirmed the fix direction works: setting `PYTHONUTF8=1` before running the script avoids the crash entirely (proves it's the default-encoding issue, not something else). Happy to send a PR with the `encoding="utf-8"` fix + the missing restore-on-failure guard if useful.

title: caveman-compress truncates target file to 0 bytes with no backup on Windows (non-ASCII content)
author: duyvo3001
body:
# Bug: `/caveman-compress` truncates target file to 0 bytes with no backup on Windows (non-ASCII content)

## Summary

On Windows, `caveman-compress` can **destroy the file it is compressing** — leaving it empty (0 bytes) with **no `.original.md` backup** — whenever the file (or the compressed output) contains any character outside cp1252 (e.g. `⚠️`, `→`, `—`, smart quotes). The file is unrecoverable unless the user has a copy elsewhere.

## Environment

- OS: Windows 11
- Python: 3.14.3
- Plugin: `caveman` @ commit `0d95a81d35a9`
- Path: no `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` set → `claude --print` CLI fallback path
- File: a personal `RTK.md` (~964 bytes) containing `⚠️` and `→`

## Repro

1. Take any prose `.md` containing a non-cp1252 char (e.g. a line with `⚠️` or an `→` arrow).
2. Run `/caveman-compress <file>` on Windows (default codepage, no `PYTHONUTF8`).
3. Observed:
   ```
   Processing: ...\RTK.md
   Compressing with Claude...
   ❌ Error: 'charmap' codec can't encode characters in position 648-649: character maps to <undefined>
   ```
4. The target file is now **0 bytes**. No `*.original.md` backup exists. Re-running then reports `❌ Refusing to compress: file is empty or whitespace-only.`

## Root cause

`scripts/compress.py` correctly pins `encoding="utf-8"` on the **subprocess** call to `claude` (lines 157–165, per the issue #152 comment) — but every **file I/O** call omits `encoding=`, so on Windows they default to cp1252:

- L249 `original_text = filepath.read_text(errors="ignore")`
- L303 `backup_path.write_text(original_text)`
- L304 `backup_path.read_text(errors="ignore")`
- L313 `filepath.write_text(compressed)`
- L331 `filepath.write_text(original_text)`  *(the "restore original" path — itself fails on Windows)*
- L340 `filepath.write_text(compressed)`

Two distinct defects follow:

1. **Data loss (0-byte file).** `Path.write_text()` opens the file in `"w"` mode, which **truncates immediately**, and *then* encodes the string. When the string contains a char cp1252 can't encode, `.write()` raises `UnicodeEncodeError` **after** truncation — leaving a 0-byte file. Because the same missing-`encoding` bug hits the backup write (L303) too, the backup either never completes or is corrupt, so there is no recovery copy. The `restore original` fallback (L331) has the identical flaw, so even the built-in recovery path fails on Windows.

2. **Silent corruption (latent).** L249 reads a UTF-8 file as cp1252 with `errors="ignore"`, producing mojibake / dropped bytes. Even when a write *doesn't* crash, the "original" that gets backed up and the body sent to Claude are already corrupted.

## Suggested fix

Add `encoding="utf-8"` to every `read_text` / `write_text` in `compress.py` — the same fix already applied to the subprocess:

```python
original_text = filepath.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="ignore")   # L249
backup_path.write_text(original_text, encoding="utf-8")                  # L303
backup_readback = backup_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="ignore")  # L304
filepath.write_text(compressed, encoding="utf-8")                        # L313, L340
filepath.write_text(original_text, encoding="utf-8")                     # L331
```

Optional hardening so a crash can never truncate the primary:
- Write to a temp file in the same dir, `fsync`, then `os.replace()` onto the target (atomic on Windows and POSIX). A failed encode then leaves the original untouched.
- Drop `errors="ignore"` on the source read, or warn — silently dropping bytes corrupts the "original" backup.

## Impact

High. Runs against a user's `CLAUDE.md`, todos, or notes — exactly the prose files the skill targets — and warehouse/dev docs routinely contain `→`, `—`, `✓`, emoji. On Windows this is a straight-up unrecoverable delete of the input.

title: Is there anyone experienced who could try running this operating-system-like project? I can provide the .md file, but I’m not getting it to work properly on my end.
author: mailtopia
body:
Could anyone help refine my prompt? I can provide the Markdown (.md) document, and I can run it again afterward. Collaboration is also welcome.

This may be an unusual request, but I believe it could serve as a meaningful benchmark for what AI-assisted software engineering is truly capable of today.

Has anyone successfully used AI to build software that tightly integrates hardware-human social networking with an operating-system-level architecture—for example, something similar to GPT-5.6 or Fable 5?

I’m asking because I’ve seen many recent discussions suggesting that AI can now generate complete software projects end-to-end within a single workflow. For more complex systems, AI-driven autonomous development (through prompting, tool loops, or agent-based workflows) is reportedly capable of completing over 70% of engineering tasks autonomously, including internal workflows at companies like Anthropic and OpenAI.

With that in mind, I’d like to ask whether anyone here would be interested in helping build the project described below.

I'm open to any implementation approach—pure prompt engineering, iterative loop-based agents, or any hybrid workflow—as long as it can move the project forward in a meaningful way.

Even partial contributions would be highly valuable. For example, implementing one or more critical subsystems—such as the domain architecture or the consensus mechanism—would already represent major progress.

You can treat the following project description as a Markdown specification, potentially including prompts and implementation guidance.

</> Markdown

Introduction
This Markdown document is intended to be used by the latest large language models (or coordinated multi-model systems) to generate a next-generation operating system.
Requirement: If current-generation models are not yet capable of fully realizing this system, development may wait for the next generation of models, and continue iteratively until completion becomes possible.
Mission and significance: To support humanity's expansion beyond Earth and enable true connectivity among all things.
For a long time, the Internet has drifted away from its original purpose. Fragmentation, platform silos, and information isolation have made frictionless access to knowledge and capability increasingly unrealistic. The ideal of invoking and orchestrating all things from a single endpoint cannot be realized through today's Internet. To move beyond its limitations, we must look beyond Earth. Space exploration presents such an opportunity. As information generated across celestial bodies reaches an unprecedented scale, today's Earth-centric Internet will inevitably be subsumed into this new networking architecture. The data accumulated on Earth will become only a small component of a much larger interplanetary information ecosystem.

Software Architecture
Core Direction：

This is an operating system designed to enable the Internet of Everything, establishing functional equality between humans and objects.
This is a space-scale operating system designed to maintain seamless information connectivity across planets, moons, space habitats, and other celestial bodies.
It is minimalistic, open-source, and designed for the most direct and frictionless possible flow of information.
Framework：

Frontend — The “Mindflow” Intelligence Layer
The frontend operates within an intelligent layer called Mindflow. Every interaction interface is dynamically predicted, generated, and rendered by AI in real time for human users. (The emphasis here is on humans, not objects)、(Mindflow functions as a unified AGI council—an orchestration layer connecting multiple large models. It operates like an “AI parliament,” where different intelligence systems deliberate, specialize, and coordinate to make decisions and provide services for humanity)
Foundation Layer — Contracts and Consensus The foundational layer operates through proposal formation and contract execution. Email-like communication acts as the immutable evidence layer for recordkeeping. Once relationships between entities are established and locked, they become the basis of consensus. Every transaction, interaction, or agreement is individually verified and cryptographically stamped.

System Layer — Grounded Consensus Network The system runs on a consensus information network whose primary grounding reference is Earth (the home planet). Earth serves as the initial grounding node of trust and synchronization. From this foundation, the network can expand infinitely while maintaining “grounded” data integrity. This creates a scalable consensus architecture capable of extending from Earth to interplanetary civilization.

Representative Scenarios (Ordered from Simple to Advanced)
The following examples are intended to help AI agents understand the intended architecture and interaction model. They progress from simple device interactions to planetary-scale coordination.

Universal Addressing
Every entity—human, device, service, organization, or celestial body—is assigned a unique mail address. Cross-domain communication is performed through these addresses. Humans and objects are treated as equal participants at the application layer while remaining isolated by clearly defined permission boundaries. Devices may optionally use hash-based email addresses as their underlying identities.

Device Communication Through Mail
A user sends a message to a light: Turn on the light.

The light executes the request and replies: The light has been turned on.

A washing machine sends its configuration dashboard to the user. After configuration, the user replies.

The house routes the message to the washing machine, which further distributes the task to its internal functional modules through its internal message bus.

The general workflow is: Device joins the network → receives an address → user communicates through mail → internal modules receive distributed messages → execution completes → confirmation is returned.

Plug-and-Play Devices
When a device joins the network:

it receives a permanent address, its identity is confirmed through consensus, it automatically contacts the manufacturer's mailbox, downloads the appropriate driver package, installs required software, upgrades firmware, and becomes immediately operational.

No manual driver installation should ever be required.

Example:

A printer connects to the system.

Instead of asking the user to install drivers, it authenticates with the manufacturer and automatically retrieves the appropriate software package.

Intelligent Document Processing
A user sends a document to a printer. The printer automatically determines the printable content from the message body or its attachments.

After printing finishes, it sends a confirmation message: Printing completed successfully.

Social Interaction Between Humans and Devices
A user may communicate simultaneously with many devices through their mailboxes. Devices can be organized into conversation groups. Users may:

assign tasks, deploy services, configure devices, or even program devices through messages.

Every device maintains a trusted communication channel with its manufacturer. If a fault occurs, the device communicates directly with the manufacturer for diagnosis and repair whenever possible.

House-Level Workflow Automation
Before the owner arrives home, the house receives a message.

The house decomposes the request into multiple subtasks and distributes them to lighting, climate control, security, entertainment, kitchen appliances, and other systems.

When the owner arrives, all devices have already completed their startup sequence according to the desired workflow.

Enterprise Workflow
A company receives an email containing a contract. The operating system automatically:

identifies individual clauses, routes each section to the responsible department, collects revisions, reconstructs the complete document, records consensus, and returns the finalized contract.

Purchase orders follow the same principle. Tasks may be routed to manufacturers, organizations, AI agents, or human specialists.

Autonomous Collaboration
Manufacturers periodically send firmware updates to devices.

Devices automatically install compatible updates. Devices also negotiate tasks directly with one another. After collaborative work is completed, the participating devices notify the owner.

Component-Level Addressability
Every component inside a vehicle has its own address. Every manufacturing event, maintenance record, and operational log is traceable through message history.

The vehicle's internal mail-communication bus functions as an internal conversation network. Collected information is continuously summarized into a real-time dashboard for the user.

Dynamic User Interfaces
No fixed graphical interface exists. Interfaces are generated on demand through AI. Every entity can expose different interfaces according to permission levels. Messages are automatically divided into:

public information, protected information, administrator-only information.

Humans similarly separate social content into public and private views.Devices expose operational dashboards while protecting sensitive controls.

Planetary Monitoring
Sensors deployed on celestial bodies continuously report environmental conditions and system status. These reports are delivered to owners and manufacturers. Authorized users may monitor or control the systems.

Unauthorized users may still access publicly available telemetry through address-based browsers and submit recommendations. No dedicated application is required.

Planetary Communication
Humans communicate directly with the Moon. Example: > Illuminate the entire lunar surface. or > Display a smiling face using lunar lighting infrastructure. Planets communicate directly with one another. Example: Mars requests temporary storage resources from Earth.

The planets jointly publish a public system announcement. The Hubble Space Telescope automatically replies: > Camera ready. Awaiting observation.

Solar-System-Scale Coordination
The entire Solar System behaves like a distributed intelligent organism. It continuously monitors environmental conditions, predicts hazards, coordinates defensive actions against threats such as asteroids, and mobilizes available infrastructure to protect life.

Collective human objectives become executable workflows spanning planets. To preserve trust and consistency, every communication path ultimately references Earth as the grounding node of the consensus network.

Universal Social Networking
Humans communicate with objects. Objects communicate with objects. Objects negotiate contracts, exchange services, perform settlements, allocate resources, and cooperate autonomously. Human-to-human communication follows the same underlying protocol.

Proposal–Contract–Consensus
Every interaction—whether between humans, machines, organizations, or celestial bodies—is represented as: Proposal → Agreement → Execution → Verification → Consensus Record. Every workflow is cryptographically stamped.

Promises become verifiable commitments rather than informal agreements.

Knowledge Engine
The operating system continuously delivers the most useful knowledge and capabilities to both humans and machines. Knowledge ranking is determined by usefulness, correctness, and long-term value rather than commercial incentives or popularity.

Every contributor—human or machine—is rewarded whenever their knowledge is reused, allowing the ecosystem to improve continuously over time.

......

Why choose an email-based communication protocol as the foundation of extreme minimalism?
Reasons:

It is a system that is inherently capable of cross-domain operation.
Common understanding (a): Email is the first application of the Internet, and its usage patterns and cognitive foundation are already deeply established.
Common understanding (b): The cross-domain nature of email addresses makes it low-cost to bridge heterogeneous systems, enabling interoperability between legacy systems and new architectures through email-based communication.

The structure of email addresses is naturally clean, uniform, and semantically domain-oriented. Humans are already familiar with it, and it has a very low cognitive entry cost.
In this context, it is suggested that it may be time to assign domains to planets.
Fast communication can be handled via real-time messaging, while slower interplanetary data exchange aligns naturally with the classical human experience of “sending and receiving letters,” achieving a design principle of extreme simplicity.

Software is email.
Reading a description or accessing a service is equivalent to contacting its email address.
Requesting functionality is done simply by sending a message.
Collaboration is achieved via forwarding. After execution, results are archived and returned via reply messages.
Functional composition is driven by AI agents that route 'mailto' requests to target entities, dynamically attaching required skills as payload packages, and returning results through a topology-based 'topia' response channel.

Everything has value, and value dynamically changes based on consensus formed around events.
Using the Ship of Theseus as an example: consensus shifts as components are replaced; value migrates as old parts form new structures. Both “ships” evolve under changing collective agreement, and no logical paradox remains under a consensus-based interpretation.

The physical world exhibits a highly structured and well-defined informational logic.
The amount of information generated by fully interconnected objects can exceed human digital social systems by several orders of magnitude, and logs are inherently more structured and precise.
Beyond consensus constraints, humans should define strict boundaries based on the semantics of mail addresses and establish cognitive red lines early in the system design.

Traditional email systems still contain security and completeness limitations.
The redesigned system, MAILTOPIA, can fully address these shortcomings and provide a more robust bridge between Earth and extraterrestrial systems.

The current trend in cross-platform technology relies heavily on AI-based fuzzy mediation, which introduces multiple intermediate layers and reduces efficiency.
A direct email-based addressing system allows AI to focus on knowledge integration and task execution, rather than acting as a fragile glue layer between systems.

Why build such an operating system
Limitations of the current Internet:

Humanity will not remain confined to Earth indefinitely. It is difficult to imagine that the current fragmented Internet—full of patches, layers of abstraction, and inefficiencies—can be extended and sustainably operated across other planets.

As humans continue to push deeper into the underlying layers of the Internet, attempting to route instructions through increasingly short and direct paths to agents, a fundamental question arises:
rather than continuously optimizing and patching the existing network, why not rebuild the system from the ground up?
A completely new networking paradigm may ultimately be more cost-efficient and scalable.

It is an essential and critical tool for celestial exploration:
Celestial exploration and development is a large-scale collaborative effort across humanity, requiring a unified and seamless information and data operating model as its foundation.

During the process of constructing and developing extraterrestrial infrastructure, disputes over ownership are inevitable. Establishing clear and immutable ownership definitions, as well as incentive mechanisms, requires a global consensus across all of humanity.

Constraints on artificial intelligence must be grounded in human consensus as the highest-level authority, in order to intervene in, balance, and mitigate undesirable early-stage tendencies.

Others:

Detailed documentation regarding human-object social interaction models and interfaces can be provided upon request. https://github.com/mailtopia/mailtopia/raw/refs/heads/main/Bit%20Object.doc

Detailed documentation regarding the consensus system for defining and partitioning the value ownership of digital-physical entities can be provided upon request. https://github.com/mailtopia/mailtopia/raw/refs/heads/main/MAILTOPIA.zip

title: miUtXZywQWvNQMmBwz6lxTubzflrj0F3
author: Dick-Hurts
body: CocT5BpDUkQQUeDsyNbkKE7WFjz4yvlE
